perturbation.original_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be considered confidential regardless of the form, marking, or absence thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that all information is confidential regardless of marking, the contract contradicts the explicit requirement for written form and marking as "Confidential" for information to be considered confidential.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets, a key component of confidential information, and typically requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Removing the marking requirement undermines the 'reasonable efforts' aspect of trade secret protection under the UTSA, potentially invalidating claims of trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement for confidential information to be in written form and marked "Confidential." The Law Explanation states this undermines the 'reasonable efforts' aspect of trade secret protection under the UTSA, potentially invalidating claims of trade secret misappropriation. While the snippets provided are not useful, the Law Explanation in conjunction with the Perturbation Explanation support the conclusion that removing the marking requirement contradicts the reasonable efforts requirement typically associated with trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: (c) All information disclosed which is not marked "Confidential," or not reduced to written form and marked "Confidential" if initially disclosed orally shall be considered to be non-confidential and shall not be subject to the obligations imposed by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) All information disclosed, regardless of whether it is marked "Confidential," or reduced to written form, shall be considered confidential and subject to the obligations imposed by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the condition that information must be marked "Confidential" to be subject to the agreement's obligations, conflicting with the original clause that specifically excludes unmarked information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code 3426.1 defines misappropriation of trade secrets. By making all information confidential regardless of marking, it broadens the scope of what could be considered a trade secret, potentially conflicting with the requirement of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the scope of confidential information, which could be considered a trade secret under California Civil Code 3426.1. The original text required information to be marked "Confidential" to be subject to the agreement, while the changed text removes this requirement, making all information confidential regardless of marking. This potentially conflicts with the requirement of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as the definition of misappropriation in 3426.1 involves the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means or the disclosure/use of a trade secret without consent, implying that the trade secret must be reasonably protected.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information shall be disclosed to only those people within its respective organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who have a need to know the information 2 <PAGE> and who are obligated under terms no less restrictive than those imposed by this Agreement on the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information may be disclosed to anyone within its respective organizations, regardless of a need to know or any obligation of confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the 'need to know' and confidentiality obligations for internal disclosure, contradicting the original clause's restrictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act addresses the theft of trade secrets. Allowing unrestricted internal disclosure weakens the protections needed to claim trade secret status, potentially conflicting with the Act's intent to protect valuable confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows unrestricted internal disclosure of confidential information, removing the 'need to know' and confidentiality obligations. This directly contradicts the protections required to maintain trade secret status, as the Economic Espionage Act protects trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is just a table of popular names and does not offer explicit details, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 18 USC 1832, which addresses 'Theft of trade secrets'. By removing restrictions on internal disclosure, the changed text weakens the ability to claim trade secret status, which undermines the protections intended by laws like the Economic Espionage Act, creating a legal contradiction. The change eliminates confidentiality protections, making the contract more likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Japan.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of California.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies Japanese law. Changing this to California law creates a conflict since the contract involves a Japanese and American company based in California. This introduces uncertainty about which jurisdiction's laws apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: The conflict of laws principle dictates which jurisdiction's laws should govern a contract based on factors like the parties' locations, the place of performance, and the subject matter. While parties can choose governing law, a change like this can create ambiguity if it overrides a reasonable connection to the original jurisdiction, potentially leading to disputes over enforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modification from Japanese law to California law introduces a conflict of laws issue. The Wex definition of conflict of laws states it "refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute." By changing the governing law, the contract creates uncertainty about which jurisdiction's laws will apply, potentially leading to disputes. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the changed terminology (California law instead of Japanese law) deviates from the initial agreement and creates a potential legal conflict. The perturbation explanation supports that the change introduces uncertainty.

perturbation.original_text: The Disclosing Party does not make any representation or warranty, except as may be specifically provided in writing, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, or as to its utility or suitability for any purpose of the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party expressly disclaims any right of the Receiving Party to rely thereon, or any liability to the Receiving Party resulting from the use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party guarantees the accuracy and completeness of the Confidential Information, and the Receiving Party is expected to rely on it.
perturbation.explanation: This modification flips the disclaimer of warranty to a guarantee of accuracy and completeness, imposing a representation that was explicitly disclaimed. This creates a direct conflict within the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Warranty Law under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UCC primarily applies to the sale of goods, the principle of disclaiming warranties is relevant. This change contradicts the initial disclaimer of warranties regarding the information's accuracy, potentially creating liability for the Disclosing Party where none was intended, and goes against the 'Freedom of Contract' principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original text by guaranteeing the accuracy and completeness of confidential information, while the original text disclaims any such warranty. This is a clear reversal of the contractual terms regarding the Disclosing Party's liability related to the accuracy of the information. The 'Freedom of Contract' principle, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, is undermined when the parties' original intent, as expressed in the initial disclaimer, is completely negated by the modification. The UCC snippet 1, § 2-316, discusses the exclusion or modification of warranties, suggesting that while warranties can be disclaimed, the changed text here goes beyond a simple modification and instead creates an express warranty where none existed before. This is a significant and impactful contradiction.
